author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do you, therefore, holy scions of our one Catholic mother, beware with all the watchfulness of which you are capable, in due submission to the Lord, of the example of crime and error such as this.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: With however great light of learning and of reputation he may shine, however much he may boast himself to be a precious stone, who endeavors to lead you after him, remember always that that brave woman who alone is lovely only to her husband, whom holy Scripture portrays to us in the last chapter of the Book of Proverbs, is more precious than any precious stones.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let no one say, I will follow such an one, for it was even he that made me a Christian; or, I will follow such an one, for it was even he that baptized me.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For "neither is he that planteth anything, neither he that watereth, but God that giveth the increase."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And "God is love; and he that dwelleth in love, dwelleth in God, and God in him."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: No one also that preaches the name of Christ, and handles or administers the sacrament of Christ, is to be followed in opposition to the unity of Christ.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: "Let every man prove his own work; and then shall he have rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For every man shall bear his own burden,"—the burden, that is, of rendering an account; for "every one of shall give an account of himself.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let us not therefore judge one another any more."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For, so far as relates to the burdens of mutual love, "bear ye one another’s burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if a man think himself to be something, when he is nothing, he deceiveth himself."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let us therefore "forbear one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace;" for no one who gathers outside that peace is gathering with Christ; but "he that gathering not with Him scattereth abroad."
